subject: First Year Topology
instructions: For Problems 1–5, show all your work and support all statements. For Problems 6–15, answer with complete definitions or statements or short proofs.

problems.text: Let \(p:E\to B\) be a covering map, and let \(p(e_0)=b_0\). Define the map \(\Phi:\pi_1(B,b_0)\to p^{-1}(b_0)\) which gives the lifting correspondence. Prove that \(\Phi\) is surjective if \(E\) is path connected, and bijective if \(E\) is simply connected.

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Show the images of the generators of the fundamental groups of the circles do not generate the fundamental group of \(H\).

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Compute the fundamental group of the connected sum of a torus and a second torus \(T^2\mathbin{\#}T^2\).

problems.text: Let \(X\) be the quotient space obtained from an 8-sided polygonal region \(P\) by pasting its edges together according to the labeling scheme \(abcdc^{-1}a^{-1}db\). It turns out that all vertices of \(P\) are mapped to the same point of the quotient space \(X\) by the pasting map. Calculate \(H_1(X)\), and using this, determine which compact surface \(X\) is homeomorphic to.
Show that if \(h,h':X\to Y\) are homotopic and \(k,k':Y\to Z\) are homotopic, then \(k\circ h\) and \(k'\circ h'\) are homotopic.
Let \(E\) and \(B\) be topological spaces. Define what it means for a function \(p:E\to B\) to be a covering map.
If \(j:A\hookrightarrow X\) is the inclusion of a retract, then show that the induced map on fundamental groups is injective.
Let \(\alpha\) be a path in \(X\) from \(x_0\) to \(x_1\). Define the map \(\hat{\alpha}:\pi_1(X,x_0)\to\pi_1(X,x_1)\) and show that \(\hat{\alpha}\) is a group isomorphism.
Let \(S^n\) be the \(n\)-sphere. Prove that \(\pi_1(S^n)\) is trivial for \(n\geq 2\).
State the Brouwer Fixed Point Theorem for the disk.

Give an example of a topological space whose fundamental group is \(\mathbb{Z}/3\mathbb{Z}\). Give an example of a topological space whose fundamental group is \(\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\).
